does: This paper introduces Long-RewardBench, a new benchmark for evaluating reward models on long-context tasks including pairwise comparison and best-of-N selection. The authors conduct empirical analysis showing that state-of-the-art generative reward models fail significantly when evaluating responses against extended context, struggling to maintain context-aware preference judgments. To address this limitation, they propose a multi-stage training strategy designed to enhance reward models' robustness in long-context scenarios, enabling them to better assess whether LLM outputs are grounded in and consistent with provided context over extended passages.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that reward modeling is central to RLHF-based alignment of large language models with human preferences. While prior reward model research has focused primarily on response-level attributes like safety and helpfulness within short contexts, this paper extends that line of work to address the long-context setting that increasingly matters in practice. It connects to recent work on evaluating and improving model robustness to longer inputs, as well as broader literature on context-aware language understanding. The paper responds to the observation that current RM evaluation benchmarks neglect the critical consistency dimension between long contexts and responses, a gap highlighted by scaling advances in both context window sizes and agent-based applications.
enables: This work enables future research in developing more sophisticated reward models that can reliably assess long-trajectory outcomes in agent systems and multi-turn interactions. By establishing Long-RewardBench as a standard evaluation framework, it opens pathways for researchers to test new architectures and training methods aimed at long-context preference modeling. The multi-stage training approach provides a foundation for scaling reward modeling to even longer contexts and more complex dependencies. The findings motivate further investigation into architectural improvements and training procedures that help models maintain consistent preferences across extended contexts, ultimately supporting better alignment and evaluation of LLMs deployed in settings requiring long-horizon reasoning and faithful grounding.
